To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how the success of Paws for Thought is measured; and (ii) any specific outcomes that have been achieved, in each year of its operation.
answer_text: The purpose of Paws for Thought is to deter the transportation of low welfare pups from Northern Ireland and progress is monitored at regular multi-agency meetings.  It should be noted that ministerial responsibilities for Paws for Thought are shared between myself and the Secretary of State for the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. Actions under the Defra Secretary of State have included refusing onward travel of pups where welfare conditions are deemed to be insufficient, suspensions of authorisations of pet transporters and ultimately, the surrender of pups.Companion animal welfare remains my policy responsibility, and through this Group, my officials have established processes to enable councils to undertake origin checks, to determine if illegal breeding has taken place.  My officials have also taken forward an awareness raising campaign, highlighting the dangers of buying a low welfare pups. A significant indicator of the Group's success is the decline in dogs and pups transported from Northern Ireland ports to Great Britain, with numbers falling by over 50%, from 16,256 in 2020 to 7,611 in 2025. A number of court prosecutions related to Paws for Thought activity have secured sentences ranging from court fines, bans on keeping animals, bans on transporting animals to custodial sentences.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any assessment made of the impact of online adult services websites operating in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any legislative or non-legislative measures available to address these websites in the facilitation of sexual exploitation.
answer_text: Telecommunications is a reserved matter for which the UK Government has responsibility and Ofcom is the regulator with responsibility for online safety in the UK.   I welcome the ongoing inquiry by the Northern Ireland All-Party Group on Modern Slavery and Commercial Sexual Exploitation (APG) on the regulation of websites that advertise prostitution.  This is a positive and proactive step that will provide valuable evidence on the impact of online adult services websites operating in Northern Ireland. I also welcome the recent publication of the Independent Anti-Slavery Commissioner’s report, ‘Behind the Profile: Sexual Exploitation and Trafficking through Adult Services Websites.’  The Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027, which I launched in July 2024, includes a range of actions to detect, investigate, disrupt and prosecute offenders of these heinous crimes.  The Strategy is available here Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-27. In this context, I look forward to receiving the findings of the APG inquiry and where evidence identifies gaps in Northern Ireland’s legal and regulatory framework, I will consider what further measures may be necessary to deter the platforms that facilitate commercial sexual exploitation.  I will also engage with the UK Government to ensure that Northern Ireland’s specific concerns are taken into account in the development of any future legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has sought legal advice on the scope of the Assembly's devolved criminal justice powers as they relate to the regulation of online platforms that may facilitate prostitution-related offences.
answer_text: The scope of the Northern Ireland Assembly’s devolved criminal justice powers does not extend to the regulation of online platforms, as this falls within the area of telecommunications, which is a reserved matter and therefore the responsibility of the UK Government. Ofcom, as the UK’s independent regulator for online safety, is responsible for ensuring full implementation of the duties set out in the Online Safety Act 2023.  In this context, therefore, I have not sought legal advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has engaged with the Home Office on whether the Online Safety Act 2023, provides adequate tools to address adult services websites.
answer_text: Telecommunications is a reserved matter for which the UK Government has responsibility.  In January 2026, I raised concerns in writing with Jess Phillips, Minister for Safeguarding and Violence Against Women and Girls at the Home Office, about the deeply disturbing issue of commercial sexual exploitation and the potential use of adult services websites to facilitate the exploitation of women and girls.I also recently welcomed the publication of the Independent Anti-Slavery Commissioner’s report, ‘Behind the Profile: Sexual Exploitation and Trafficking through Adult Services Websites’ in which she makes recommendations for both the UK Government and Ofcom. I look forward to receiving the findings of the Northern Ireland All-Party Group on Modern Slavery and Commercial Sexual Exploitation’s inquiry into the regulation of websites which advertise prostitution and to further engagement in due course with the Home Office on the recommendations arising from both these inquiries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department has made, or will make in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate, to introduce Slavery and Trafficking Risk Orders, which were identified as a commitment under the Northern Ireland Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027.
answer_text: I remain committed to the introduction of Slavery and Trafficking Risk Orders during this Assembly mandate and the lifetime of the Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027.  Following the publication of the Strategy in July 2024, my officials have initiated engagement with operational partners in Northern Ireland, as well as with colleagues in UK and Scottish Government, to best consider an appropriate legislative and implementation approach.Officials are progressing the development of legislative instruments to strengthen Northern Ireland’s response to modern slavery and human trafficking, with preliminary instruments to be presented to the Assembly shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any estimate her Department has made of the number of cases per year in Northern Ireland, that might warrant the application of a Slavery and Trafficking Risk Order.
answer_text: My Department anticipates that there will be a small number of expected cases which might warrant the application of a Slavery and Trafficking Risk Order in Northern Ireland following implementation, based on available comparative data for England and Wales.  Officials continue to engage with operational partners, including the PSNI and PPS, as well as with colleagues in UK and Scottish Government to determine the practical implications of the introduction of Slavery and Trafficking Risk Orders in Northern Ireland including a more detailed estimate of prevalence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any plans his Department has to increase capacity at Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Saintfield, to facilitate Key Stage 2 learners.
answer_text: Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Belfast offers special education provision for children up to primary 4.   The school’s second site was established in the 2023/24 school year on the site of the former St Mary’s Primary School in Saintfield.  The Education Authority (EA) carried out the first phase of refurbishment works to provide three classrooms and ancillary space as well as the removal of a double modular unit which was no longer fit for purpose and the school began using the accommodation in January 2024.A second phase of works to deliver a double modular classroom with associated ancillary space completed in October 2024.  The EA has advised that the Belfast site has capacity for some new Nursery and Primary 1 pupils for this September. The Saintfield site accommodates pupils from Primary 1 to Primary 4 and has no additional capacity for September 2026.I have repeatedly stated my commitment to strategically plan to help address the future needs of children with Special Educational Needs (SEN). I announced a capital investment programme for SEN on 30 April 2024 however there are currently no plans to extend provision in Oakwood beyond primary 4. As with children on the Belfast site, following a child’s annual review at the agreed point of transition the EA will work closely with parents to secure a new school placement to best meet their needs at that time.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of any actions that the UK Government is taking to ensure that implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, for patients with diabetes in Northern Ireland does not fall behind implementation in England.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices, in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan. Similar to other nations, a phased 5-year implementation plan is in the final stages of development, in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Funding has been secured to support the implementation of this plan through the Regional Insulin Pump service.  This will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit. However, the most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to reform the current contractual and reimbursement framework so it better reflects real acquisition costs and distributes supply chain risk more equitably, given ongoing underfunding of community pharmacy and the financial risks created by medicine price volatility and Drug Tariff clawbacks.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to ensure fair, accurate and prompt payment for HSC commissioned pharmaceutical services. To provide stability to the community pharmacy sector and ensure the continued supply of medicines to patients, the Department has invested significantly in recent years. Since April 2018, funding for community pharmacy has increased by almost £60m (57%). This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK.  Furthermore, the Minister recently approved an inflationary uplift of 4% for 2025/26 which equates to £4.72m. A backdated payment has been made to community pharmacies in February which relates to the period April 2025 to January 2026. Two further monthly instalments will be made in March and April 2026. Discount clawback is a mechanism designed to modulate margin as part of medicines reimbursement arrangements. The annual margin survey ensures that, on average, community pharmacy reimbursement arrangements cover the cost of purchasing medicines plus a level of margin meaning that HSC dispensing should be a profitable activity overall. The actual annual margin achieved by community pharmacies in Northern Ireland in recent years has significantly exceeded the minimum threshold. Unlike other UK regions, any excess in margin is not recouped.  For 2025/26, the retained minimum margin threshold was increased from £31.5m to £34m.In regard to financial risk created by medicines price volatility, where significant price instability occurs, then a “concessionary price” is activated to help reset the price. Furthermore, all concessionary drugs have been Zero Discount since April 2024 which means that discount is not deducted from these products.In Northern Ireland, unlike other parts of the UK, there is also a £3,000 advance payment for concessionary drugs which remains under review. Given the recent concerns from contractors in relation to the financial pressures arising from current price fluctuations for a number of medicines officials are currently taking steps to review a potential increase in the special advance payable to contractors. The current community pharmacy contractual arrangements in Northern Ireland centre around a funding envelope determined by the Department and services commissioned by SPPG, in line with strategic priorities, and developed in collaboration with CPNI. These arrangements have benefited community pharmacy, with year-on-year increases to recurrent funding, annual pay uplifts aligned with DDRB and exceptional investments such as for National Insurance increases.  As such I see no reason to change these arrangements at this time. My Department will continue to liaise with CPNI, through the range of established fora and remains committed to working constructively with the sector to ensure that community pharmacies are supported within a fair and sustainable framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps he is taking to address ongoing medicine shortages, including the clinical and operational pressures they are placing on community pharmacies; and (ii) any steps he will take to ensure that vulnerable patients continue to have reliable access to essential medicines.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and others in the supply chain to ensure patients have fair and equitable access to treatments across the UK. There are over 14,000 licensed medicines in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. Community pharmacies in Northern Ireland (NI) have access to these medicines through numerous pharmaceutical wholesalers and medicine licence holders as part of the UK-wide medicine supply chain.However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand globally. I recognise the impact medicine shortages can have on patients and healthcare teams in NI, including the clinical and operational pressures these can have on community pharmacy teams. When a medicine is not available to meet overall patient demand, there are well-established tools and processes that can be applied to assist healthcare teams and ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments. These tools and processes include but are not limited to providing HSC healthcare professionals, including community pharmacy teams, with advice and guidance on the actions they can take, placing medicines on a list to prevent their export from the UK or from being hoarded by wholesalers, and the importation of medicines from other markets to meet patients’ needs.My Department has also recently published information to assist patients on steps they can take if they are experiencing medicine supply disruptions. This information can be found via https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/medicine-shortages.While these tools and processes are well-established and help to ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments during periods of supply disruptions, I had previously written to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK. My Department is committed to working with our partners across the UK to find strategies to improve the security and supply of essential medicines to the UK to ensure patients have reliable access to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate stabilisation funding he will provide to community pharmacies to prevent further financial deterioration and potential closures, particularly in rural areas.
answer_text: Firstly, I would like to acknowledge the vital role that community pharmacists and their teams play across Northern Ireland, including in rural areas where they are often the only accessible healthcare provider within several miles. My Department recognises that maintaining sustainable access to pharmaceutical services in rural communities is critical to health system resilience and to equitable access to care.My Department has a statutory duty to remunerate providers of health service commissioned pharmaceutical services in a fair, accurate and prompt manner. To provide stability to the community pharmacy sector my Department has invested significantly in recent years. Since April 2018, funding for community pharmacy has increased by almost £60m (57%). This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK. Further to this I recently approved an inflationary uplift of 4% for 2025/26, which equates to £4.72m, to support pharmacy contractors in relation to inflation-linked increases in operating costs. A backdated payment has recently been made to community pharmacies in February 2026 which relates to the period April 2025 to January 2026. Two further monthly instalments will be made in March and April 2026.Within the 2025/26 overall financial package, £1.1m recurrent funding remains ringfenced through the Rural Pharmacy Access Scheme to support pharmacies serving smaller or isolated populations. This scheme has operated since 2011 and specifically recognises the importance of sustaining access in rural communities where alternative provision may be limited or absent.The Northern Ireland Drug Tariff sets out the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by community pharmacies. Where there is significant price instability or price fluctuations in-month for medicines, then a “concessionary price” is activated to help reset the price.  Furthermore, since 1 April 2024 all drugs awarded concessionary prices have been subject to Zero Discount, i.e. discount (or clawback) is not deducted from these products. In Northern Ireland, unlike other parts of the UK, there is also a special advance in the system for drugs on concession which is currently set at £3,000 per contractor.  I have listened to the recent concerns from contractors in relation to the financial pressures arising from current price fluctuations for a number of medicines, and in order to support the network, my Department is taking steps to review the special advance payable to contractors. My Department remains committed to working constructively with the sector to ensure that community pharmacies are supported within a fair and sustainable framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of times attractions in the Strangford constituency were featured or promoted by Tourism Ireland, in the last five years.
answer_text: Extensive Tourism Ireland programmes of promotion overseas have taken place for many years highlighting Strangford on the global stage. Local Strangford businesses benefit from important platforms organised by Tourism Ireland for connecting with international tour operators, most recently with the Mourne Gullion Strangford UNESCO Geopark’s participating in ‘Celtic Connections’ in Glasgow in January this year. Travel trade familiarisation visits, including the ‘Meet the Buyer 2025’ event in March, brought in 129 top international buyers for an ‘Embrace the Great Outdoors’ themed familiarisation programme that included Strangford Lough Activity Centre. In October, seven North American tour operators specialising in outdoor holidays visited Tracey’s Farmhouse Kitchen.International publicity for Strangford includes ITV1’s ‘Tour de Fred: Northern Ireland’, which featured the Mourne Mountains and Strangford Lough. In November 2025, five international journalists explored County Down and Strangford Lough during Van Morrison’s 80th birthday celebrations, including Coney Island, Downpatrick, Strangford Lough, Killyleagh and Ardglass. In March 2024, Tourism Ireland’s campaign with The Telegraph and Rory Best showcased County Down’s hidden gems, like Killowen Distillery and Strangford Lough. In June 2024, a digital campaign saw German actor Uke Bosse visit sites including Castle Ward, with four short videos reaching hundreds of thousands of German viewers online.After the release of Dungeons and Dragons: Honor Among Thieves in 2023, Tourism Ireland invited top journalists to visit some of the locations like Castle Ward and the Ards Peninsula. This representation saw coverage in leading newspapers, websites, lifestyle and entertainment magazines and the Access Hollywood TV programme.In March 2023 tour operators took part in a Coastal Adventures tour which included a canoeing trip on Strangford Lough and a traditional baking lesson in Tracey’s Farmhouse Kitchen outside Newtownards.In 2023 Dutch and German travel experts explored the area and included a ‘canoe and cake’ wellness experience on Strangford Lough and visits to Castle Ward and Hinch Distillery.Strangford Lough was also included in the itinerary for 22 high profile American travel agents in October 2022.In addition, Tourism Ireland promotes Strangford to over 7.5 million social media followers on Instagram, Facebook and TikTok.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total spend on the York Street Interchange Project, to date.
answer_text: The total spend on the York Street Interchange Project to the end of the 2024/25 financial year was £25,750k. A budget allocation of £40k is available in the current financial year; however, expenditure is currently not finalised.           In October 2022 my predecessor published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review report and asked officials to take forward further work to refine the options within it. Officials have completed this work and have engaged with key stakeholders on the York Street Interchange project, to gather their assessment of the report.Officials have presented the findings of this work to me, and I am taking the time to consider the next steps for this key infrastructure project.As the scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship project, I have asked my officials to consider possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate the scheme progression. This work is at an early stage and will take several months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any measures and performance indicators being used under the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan to ensure that reductions in waiting lists translate into completed treatments and improved patient outcomes, rather than additional consultations or assessments.
answer_text: Despite the challenges in relation to funding and workforce capacity, we continue to make steady progress under the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan.Importantly, my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group is actively scrutinising how funding in the Plan is being utilised by Trusts to ensure maximum value for money and better patient outcomes. Reductions in waiting lists delivered through the Plan are driven by a combination of additional treatments/appointments, assessments and diagnostics, and validation.Since April 2025, over 200,000 additional time critical or red-flag procedures have been delivered. We are also seeing significant reductions in waits across a range of specialties. From March 2025 to January 2026, there has been an overall decrease of 79% across named procedures. This includes 99% in colonoscopy, 89% in tonsillectomy, 82% in primary hips, 81% in laparoscopic cholecystectomies, and 79% in paediatric scopes.Primary care elective services have delivered almost 9,000 procedures since April 2025, reducing demand on hospital settings and supporting earlier intervention for patients. There has been measurable progress in reducing waits for assessment/diagnostic services: endoscopy waiting lists have reduced by 65% since June 2022, including a 32% reduction since March 2025, while routine waits of over nine weeks for CT and MRI scans have reduced by 27% and 19% respectively.Alongside this, validation work ensures that lists are accurate by removing patients who no longer need or want their surgery, freeing up capacity for those who do. More than 49,000 outpatient pathways have undergone administrative or clinical validation since April 2025.In parallel, we continue to support patients while they wait through Waiting Well initiatives and the Cancer Charities Grant Scheme. Taken together, these initiatives are helping to ensure that reductions in waiting lists are translating into completed treatments and improved patient outcomes for people in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Type 2 diabetes is a preventable health condition, to detail any plans to (i) release funding for type 2 diabetes; (ii) improve access to public health information; (iii) ensure timely access to early diagnosis and intervention programmes; and (iv) improve access to diabetes technology.
answer_text: I recognise that diabetes is one of the most challenging chronic conditions facing people in Northern Ireland and impacts both at an individual and population level.  Over the past few years, the NI Diabetes Network undertook both qualitative and quantitative research, working with all key stakeholders, to support the development of a Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes.  Subsequently, a Community Care model for Type 2 Diabetes was commissioned in 2024. This model aims to provide additional support and intervention to those individuals living with Type 2 Diabetes who need additional input to manage, stabilise or control their condition and improve their self-management. In terms of public health information and prevention of Type 2 diabetes, this work is led by the Public Health Agency.  The Diabetes Prevention Programme is an evidence-based diabetes prevention programme for Northern Ireland, which was previously commissioned and implemented by the Public Health Agency across all five Health and Social Care Trusts.  Although this programme was not recurrently funded, Health and Social Care Trusts continue to deliver the programme at a reduced volume.The new obesity strategic framework, “Healthy Futures” was published on 26th November 2025. The framework provides an umbrella for actions to prevent and address overweight and obesity, from education and prevention through to weight management and treatment services. Previous obesity strategies focused solely on prevention. The Public Health Agency invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes that align to diabetes prevention and continues to work with a range of other partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention.    In 2022, the NI Diabetes Network facilitated the development of a Regional Pathway for the Managed Access of Glucose Monitoring for adults living with Type 2 diabetes.  Access to these devices should be in line with NICE criteria, with the most appropriate system for each individual the responsibility for the clinician to determine, in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding that has been allocated for the implementation of continuous glucose monitoring and TA943 guidance for patients with diabetes, in the last 18 months.
answer_text: There are many Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices available to support people living with diabetes.  The most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.Funding for Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices which are not listed on the NI Drug Tariff would need to come directly from the Trusts or privately funded by the patient. Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices, in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan. Similar to other nations, a phased 5-year implementation plan is in the final stages of development, in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Funding has been secured to support the implementation of this plan through the Regional Insulin Pump service.  This will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Northern Ireland is the only UK region without a national diabetes audit, for an update on any plans for the publication of a national diabetes audit.
answer_text: The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales. There have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit. The NI Diabetes Network is working with Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland colleagues and Diabetes UK to review and consider the information that can be collated from across the Health and Social Care system to support participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  Work is ongoing to support the development of a Diabetes Audit for Northern Ireland, as an initial stage, however the Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.

answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices, in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Similar to other nations, a phased 5-year implementation plan is in the final stages of development, in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Funding has been secured to support the implementation of this plan through the Regional Insulin Pump service.  This will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.However, the most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the HSC Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to increase and retain staffing levels, within the DFI Southern Region.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant recruitment and retention challenges, in particular for specialist roles, such as Civil Engineers. This impacts our ability to deliver essential services and much needed infrastructure projects. To strengthen our workforce planning arrangements internally, the Department has established a People Sub-Committee ensuring that workforce capacity risks are identified and managed. At present, there are 11 recruitment campaigns specifically focussed on filling vacancies within the Transport, Roads and Asset Maintenance group which cover posts in the Southern Region.  These competitions are at various stages of the process and in the last 12 months our efforts have led to approximately 120 appointments, with a further 100 anticipated soon for posts across the Transport, Roads and Asset Maintenance group.Building on the success of the Talentdog recruitment exercise delivered by the NICS in the North-West, my officials are working to advertise the recruitment of industrial staff using this fast-track approach. Officials also plan to use the already successfully established Academy approach, to recruit new industrial staff. Both these will increase the level of staff resource available to repair potholes and carry out other important road maintenance functions.Southern Division Maintenance currently has 22 Full Time Equivalent (FTE) vacancies.  Southern Division Traffic currently has 9.6 FTE vacancies. FTE takes into account staff on alternative working patterns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has given any consideration to changing her Department's policy that, once a pothole is marked yellow, you can no longer claim compensation, particularly if surface conditions such as pooling or ponding of water, conceals those markings and subsequent damage occurs.
answer_text: An individual may make a claim for compensation for damage to their vehicle regardless of whether a defect is spray-painted or marked out for repair. Defects which are deemed to be actionable under my Department’s Road Maintenance Standards for Safety are marked out or spray-painted by highway inspectors, where it is safe for the inspector to do so. The purpose of the marking is to highlight the location of the required repair to our internal or external contractor. If it is not safe to do so due to traffic volumes etc, the inspector is only required to provide clear instructions to facilitate the identification of the precise location of the defect requiring repair.Each claim made against my Department is investigated on its own merits. If the investigation confirms my Department is not legally liable for the damage being claimed, the case will be rejected.  Legal liability is ultimately decided by the courts. However, the courts’ consideration of a claim will include whether my Department’s Road Maintenance Standards for Safety were adhered to. Although the marking out or spray-painting of a defect doesn’t remove liability for a roads related public liability claim, my officials will consider, inter alia, whether the associated repair response time was met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale behind the disposal of unopened medication within care homes when the medication is no longer required following the patients death.
answer_text: Once medicines are dispensed to a patient, they cannot be reused by the health service. Where medicines have left a pharmacy, it is not possible to assure the quality of returned medicines on physical inspection alone. When medicines are returned from patients’ homes or Care Homes, there is no way of guaranteeing that the medicines have been stored or handled appropriately. This could affect patient safety. Therefore, reuse of returned medicines is not undertaken in Northern Ireland or any of the UK regions.However, the Department is leading a programme on Valuing Medicines which aims to improve access to medicines, improve patient safety, avoid taking unnecessary medicines, and reduce wastage. One of the key strands in this programme is to reduce wastage generated from over-prescribing of medicines which happens when people receive treatments they don’t need, no longer use, or don’t want. Actions can be taken by individuals to stop ordering items they don’t need, as promoted in the ‘Don’t Use It, Don’t Order It’ public awareness campaign (2024). In addition, efficient procedures for handling repeat prescriptions, medicines reconciliation on admission and discharge from hospital, and medication reviews in primary care are being promoted to reduce overprescribing, ensure appropriate quantities are supplied, and minimise any wastage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action that has been taken to reduce waiting times for patients seeking dermatology outpatient care.
answer_text: In dermatology, several waiting list initiatives (WLI) are underway to manage red flag demand and improve waiting times. Approximately £4.7m has been earmarked for dermatology waiting list initiatives during Q1-Q3 of 2025/26. During April 2025 to September 2025 this has resulted in a spend totalling £2.3m.  This spend has delivered almost 6,100 dermatology new/review outpatient assessments and day case procedures.The Regional Dermatology Forum continues to meet to consider medium- and long-term solutions, including addressing workforce challenges, exploring alternative pathways to mitigate clinical manpower shortfalls, and identifying opportunities to increase capacity within each Trust.Primary and Secondary Care services are also working collaboratively to reduce waiting times and improve access.  Ensuring that patients are seen in the right place by the right professional. This includes:In Primary Care Elective Services:• Investment to deliver 4,410 face-to-face and remote consultations annually for patients presenting with routine dermatology conditions such as adult and childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes, and suspected actinic keratosis.• A pilot launched in March 2025 which enables GPs with enhanced skills to surgically treat patients diagnosed with low-risk basal cell carcinomas in primary care.• The Regional Dermatology Photo Triage Pathway, which supports image capture and e-referral for suspected skin cancer and urgent basal cell carcinoma cases. As of October 2025, 90% of GP practices are contracted to provide this pathway, enabling effective decision-making at the point of triage in secondary care.In Secondary Care Services:• Development of specialist nursing roles to assess and treat patients;• Use of biologic medications to manage chronic dermatological conditions;• Introduction of virtual and telephone consultations to provide advice and guidance and reduce waiting times;• Implementation of Patient-Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU) services and enhanced triage clinics to reform outpatient care.Performance is managed at the provider level, ensuring that activity aligns with investment and that waiting lists are addressed within available resources. However, it is acknowledged that reducing waiting times is a long term challenge. Sustained investment over five years will be required to bring hospital waiting times to acceptable levels. I hope this information is helpful in addressing your query.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the commitment by the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Health and Social Care to implement a 24/7 thrombectomy service in England by April 2026, whether he plans a similar timeframe for the roll out of a 24/7 service in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware of the commitment made by the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Health and Social Care to implement a 24/7 thrombectomy service in England by April 2026. This commitment did not form part of the discussion when I met with the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care recently as the expansion of the thrombectomy service in Northern Ireland (NI) has already been identified as a priority in my Department’s Stroke Action Plan.I recognise that people affected by stroke here have a right to access to services and treatment consistent with availability in England. In this context, I remain committed to the expansion of the thrombectomy service in Northern Ireland from the current hours of operation to a full 24/7 service.  Following approval of the final budget for 2025/26, my Department was left with a funding gap of over £600 million. Therefore, I am currently unable to allocate the required additional funding. Consequently, at this point, I am unable to commit to a timescale for 24/7 thrombectomy in NI.In the interim, I am committed to maximising access to ensure that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

answer_text: My Department has a number of effective measures in place to ensure that all those who need to make an application for Disability Benefits receive clear, timely and accessible communication about their disability benefit applications. These include:             Advice about making an application is available from the respective disability benefit telephony teams.The provision of written communications in alternative formats, including braille, British and Irish Sign Language, large print and audio CD.  A video relay service is also available.The Department’s Make the Call service can provide benefit advice and assistance with application form completion and can arrange a home visit. Benefit branches also signpost to independent advice organisations.Information is available on NI Direct for each of the disability benefits which is regularly reviewed and updated. In addition, my Department has ongoing engagement with key stakeholders who advocate for families and individuals in these circumstances, to escalate urgent cases and identify areas for service improvement. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support and advice offered to children and young people with cancer, and their families, during the waiting period before the award of Personal Independence Payment and Disability Living Allowance to help mitigate the immediate financial challenges that come after a diagnosis of cancer.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with stakeholders to ensure that families with a child or young person with cancer can access the support they need.  As a result there are established escalation points for the Royal Victoria Hospital Children's Oncology and Haematology Social Work team and other support organisations to fast track new claims where there is urgent need. The Department’s Make the Call Service is also available to support families by providing benefit advice, assistance with application form completion and signposting to other available support.  Where required this can be done as a home visit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any specific measures in place to reduce waiting times for Personal Independence Payment and Disability Living Allowance  awards for children and young people diagnosed with cancer; and (ii) how these measures are being monitored and evaluated.
answer_text: My Department has established escalation points for the Royal Victoria Hospital Children's Oncology and Haematology Social Work team and other support organisations to fast-track new claims where there is urgent need. In addition, there are special rules in place for someone who is terminally ill and has a life expectancy of less than 12 months to ensure that DLA or PIP can be paid more quickly, normally in under 4 days. My Department’s average processing time for new applications for Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children in the 2024/2025 year was 35.8 working days*. The latest Personal Independence Payment (PIP) statistics, published in May 2025, indicate that the average time taken to process a new application to PIP was 17 weeks. (*The DLA figures above are taken from operational management information.)DLA and PIP managers monitor the average processing time for claims on an ongoing basis using weekly and monthly performance statistics which are extracted from the relevant benefit systems. New claim award and payment processes are prioritised for completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to improve access to specialist migraine services in (i) community; and (ii) primary care, settings.
answer_text: I recognise that Neurology services, including the specialist migraine service, are under considerable pressure. In response to those pressures my Department commissioned a Regional Review of Neurology.The final report of the Review, which has been the subject of a recent public consultation, sets out four priorities for improvement which are underpinned by supporting recommendations. A number of these recommendations, including growing the Neurology workforce, new ways of working and the development of condition-specific pathways would have a positive impact on pressures in migraine services.  My officials are currently undertaking an analysis of responses to the public consultation to inform next steps. However, I must be clear that full implementation will require significant additional funding.  While I am committed to carefully considering the need for additional funding, the pace of implementation may have to be adjusted in line with the funding available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department has taken to ensure, as far as possible and notwithstanding appropriate protection for minors, transparency is a feature of family court proceedings.
answer_text: I believe that greater transparency regarding decisions made in the family courts would help improve public understanding of the process and enhance confidence in the family justice system. However, this needs to be balanced with protecting the anonymity of the children and families involved.Following policy scoping work carried out by my Department to consider the potential to increase the transparency of family courts, I discussed this issue with the Lady Chief Justice, who indicated that she would consider further. Following from that, the Judicial Family Working Group recently launched a targeted consultation on its plans for a media reporting pilot in the Family Division of the High Court, which is planned to run from October 2025 to June 2026.I welcome the proposed pilot, as it will be an important step in informing consideration of how best to achieve greater openness and transparency of the family courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what mental health support her Department provides to fathers, engaged in the family court system, who are not the subject of a criminal conviction.
answer_text: I recognise that legal proceedings concerning arrangements for children can be stressful and emotive for the parents involved and the challenges that fathers may experience when engaging with the family court system. However, the provision of specific mental health support is a matter for the Minister of Health. I am aware that some support is  available to fathers in certain circumstances, through organisations such as the Men’s Advisory Project, which also provides counselling services. In the context of domestic abuse, I acknowledge that there are gaps in the provision of services to meet the needs of different individuals, including men and boys.  Recent research by the Commissioner for Victims of Crime has provided valuable insights that will help my Department, working in partnership with other relevant Departments, to address these gaps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the organisations that her Department provides a financial contribution to, which offer support for fathers involved within the family court process.
answer_text: Based on information available for the current 2025-26 financial year, my Department and its agencies have not made a financial contribution to organisations which offer support for anyone involved in the family court process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Office of AI and Digital.
answer_text: Since its launch in June, the Office has made good progress. Its vision is to create a cross-cutting strategy for the ethical adoption of Artificial Intelligence across our departments.To support this, we have set up two key groups:• An internal AI stakeholder group, with representation from all nine departments; and• An external reference group, which offers expert advice, scrutiny, and guidance to ensure our strategy is both practical and deliverable.		Working closely with our partners means we can shape a strategy that is forward-looking and meets the evolving needs of departments.Professor McCarthy and her team are now preparing the strategy framework, based on feedback received so far. This will go through further consultation before we move to a full department-wide strategy and action plan.This is a vital step in ensuring that we are ready to harness the benefits of AI responsibly and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to increase the number of nursing posts available to ensure all local graduates can avail of a position.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline any additional focus that can be placed on the role of parents and primary care givers, within the current strategies to address educational underachievement, to ensure they are equipped to support the work of educators.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the second call for projects as part of transformation being overseen by the Public Sector Transformation Board.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any representations made to the UK Government in relation to removing all barriers to trade and ensuring unfettered access within the UK internal market.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what procedures her Department follows to notify local businesses of road closures, in the event of scheduled maintenance.
answer_text: My Department does not notify road closures directly to local businesses. Officials process between 25,000 and 30,000 road or lane closure applications annually through a recently developed IT system which sends an email notification to a council-based distribution list in advanced of planned roadworks.  These distribution lists include the local council, blue-light services, coastal and transportation services. Upcoming roadworks on the busiest routes are also advertised in the main press.Given the volume of closures, and in line with the Limited Service principles being implemented, communications with road users in relation to upcoming roadworks focus on the busiest routes, primarily the 'A' Class network.These communications include the use of advanced notification or Variable Message signage on the impacted route in advance of the works and notification on TrafficWatchNI, with priority given to those roadworks which have the greatest impact on the travelling public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what plans her Department has to increase the number of days a temporary use of land, under permitted development rights, can be used to bring this in line with the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Part 5 Class B of the Schedule to the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 (GPDO) allows for the temporary use of land or buildings for up to 28 days in a calendar year, subject to certain conditions and limitations, without requiring a planning application (the 28-day rule). I have no current plans to increase the number of days permitted for the temporary use of land under the GPDO.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has plans to implement vehicle tax exemption or reduction for those in receipt of Attendance Allowance, to bring this in line with those in receipt of Personal Independent Payment.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty is a reserved matter and policy responsibility, including that relating to exemptions, sits with the Westminster Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans his Department has to continue funding for the Small Pockets of Deprivation programme, for the remainder of the financial year 2025-26.
answer_text: The Small Pockets of Deprivation (SPoD) programme, which forms part of the Executives wider People and Place Strategy: A strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal (2003) is currently managed through the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) via a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) agreed annually.You will be aware from my recent budget announcement; I announced that I have provided an additional £2.8 million for the Voluntary and Community Sector (VCS). This represents an uplift of 9% in revenue funding for the sector based on 2024/25 funding. This uplift applies to all the VCS organisations supported via the People and Place Strategy including those in the SPoD programme.A review of the Executives People and Place Strategy has been completed and I anticipate that the draft Strategic Framework document will be submitted for my consideration during 2025 and, subject to my agreement and that of my Executive colleagues, a transition period will commence. There is, however, no expectation of any changes within the existing strategy taking place until at least 2026/27 financial year.To that end, a Memorandum of Understanding has been agreed with the NIHE and they will continue to manage the SPoD programme as previously.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what funding will be made available to communities wishing to celebrate the 250th Anniversary of the signing of the US Declaration of Independence and Ulster-Scots links to this historic event.
answer_text: I announced £250k earlier this year for organisations to mark the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, and my officials are working on the development of the funding scheme.It will be several months before it will be ready to receive applications. A further announcement will be made when preparations are completed, and the scheme opens to applicants.The Department for Communities provides around £2.1m in annual funding to the Ulster-Scots Agency, who have the legislative responsibility for the promotion of greater awareness of Ulster Scots cultural issues. The Ulster-Scots Agency are also developing proposals that celebrate the cultural links to this historic 250th anniversary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on progress to ensure faith-based organisations can avail of Departmental funding to support communities.
answer_text: My Department invests £75k annually as part of the Regional Infrastructure Support Programme (RISP) to promote the role of faith-based organisations in serving the needs of communities across Northern Ireland. Grant funding is provided to the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NICVA) and the Rural Community Network (RCN) for convening the Community Faiths’ Forum and delivery of outcomes in relation to faith-based community development work. Work to refresh our Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support Policy Framework is progressing.  As part of ongoing investment in infrastructure support, my Department will continue to champion the Community Faiths’ Forum and make future funding accessible to faith-based organisations, provided they meet the relevant eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any schemes his Department intends to fund, subsequent to the Spending Review, to (i) tackle fuel poverty; and (ii) investment in improving domestic energy efficiency.
answer_text: I can confirm that while the Northern Ireland Executive will receive Barnett consequential because of Spending Review decisions, this funding is unhypothecated, which in simplest terms means the Executive has discretion to determine how funding is allocated. Unhypothecated funding such as Barnett, forms part of the Executive’s overall funding pot, meaning funds are available to use in line with the Executive’s needs and priorities.I remain committed to tackling fuel poverty, and to that end will bring a final Fuel Poverty Strategy to the Executive before the end of the year and consult on the new Warm Healthy Homes Scheme early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of taxi policy and legislation, particularly the Class C taxi licensing system, in order to align the legislation with Great Britain.
answer_text: Since I announced the phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing, I have agreed the Terms of Reference and Officials have initiated the first two phases, which have included identifying the key requirements of the review, mapping out the current licensing system and carrying out research into how wedding and funeral cars are licensed in other jurisdictions. In addition, I have held some meetings with key stakeholders, including representatives of the wedding car sector and the wider taxi industry. These meetings have provided a useful insight into the concerns and views of those within the Class C taxi licensing category and those taxi operators working in the wider taxi industry. Officials are also planning on issuing a sampling survey later this month to gather further information from the taxi industry.The outcome of this review may not necessarily align our legislation with Britain.It is planned that this review should be completed by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the work of the Chief Scientific and Technology Advisor in implementing tech-based solutions in public service reform.
answer_text: Delivering on the commitment made in the Programme for Government, we tasked the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser with establishing the Office of AI and Digital. The new office was launched on 11th June and will play a central role in shaping our digital future. The CSTA will work closely with an Advisory Panel and the Government AI Network to co-develop a comprehensive Strategy and Action Plan that will guide us through to 2030. The plan will include advice to departments on the integration of tech-based solutions for public sector reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the scope of her amendment to the Justice Bill regarding provision for a deepfake offence.
answer_text: On 29 April, I announced in the Assembly my intention to table an amendment to the Justice Bill, at Consideration Stage, to provide for Northern Ireland specific offences to ensure all aspects of offending behaviour in this area are captured. I also announced that there would be a public consultation seeking views on legislative proposals. This will be launched in the coming weeks. Responses to the consultation will determine the scope of the draft clauses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the project to erect permanent environmental signage on publicly owned land at the southern part of Portavogie dunes.
answer_text: NIEA does not have a signage policy or powers under the Environment (NI) Order 2002 to erect permanent signage within or outside an ASSI. However, NIEA officials have been engaging, and will continue to, with Ards and North Down Borough Council (ANDBC) to erect permanent environmental signage at or near the southern part of Portavogie dunes on publicly owned land. DAERA will continue to seek a solution to this matter with ANDBC and others.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when a school that has received a discontinuance notification is notified of its closing date.
answer_text: Any significant change proposed to a school, such as a proposal for discontinuance, requires the publication of a statutory Development Proposal (DP).  The implementation date for every DP is clearly set out in the proposal.Each DP is assessed on its merits and a report is then provided to me by my officials for my consideration and ultimate decision.  When I make my decision on a DP, my officials immediately verbally inform the proposer and where applicable the sponsoring body (e.g. NICIE, CnaG etc).  Where the proposer of a DP is not the school itself but a school’s managing authority, the managing authority immediately then informs the school.My decision is subsequently confirmed in writing to the proposer, the Education Authority, as publisher of all proposals and the sponsoring body, as appropriate.When a proposal has been approved, it must be implemented in full.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline when hybrid closed loop systems will be made available for diabetes patients across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. This plan should provide equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of additional public parking by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust at the Royal Victoria Hospital site.
answer_text: In August 2024 the Belfast Health & Social Care (HSC) Trust undertook a scoping exercise for the addition of a modular style car park on the Royal Victoria Hospital site. Trust staff met with Department of Health officials in October 2024 to discuss delivery options and potential for capital funding.As part of planning discussions between the Belfast HSC Trust and Belfast City Council Planning Service for redevelopment of the Royal Hospitals site in 2018, the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) Road Service, a statutory consultee, did not believe there was sufficient justification to increase parking on the site.In advance of developing more detailed proposals for modular style parking, the Trust are engaging with the Planning Service to determine the likelihood of achieving the required planning approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what support is available for teachers impacted by school discontinuance.
answer_text: The discontinuance of a school is a difficult time for school-based staff and the employing authorities provide support throughout the process. On the notification of the decision to close a school, the employing authorities engage initially with the Principal to advise on the decision and to provide information and support through a meeting with teaching staff.  At the meeting with staff, human resources officers from the employing authority and union representatives are present to provide group and individual sessions.  Through the Teachers’ Negotiation Committee (TNC) a collective agreement, TNC 2013/2 has been agreed which ensures that teaching staff are protected from compulsory redundancy for two years from the date of a school closure. In addition to this a transferred redundancy process is operated across all the education sector whereby all eligible teachers in the school which is closing are afforded the opportunity, if they so wish, to transfer to an alternative school with the agreement of the respective Board of Governors. If they do not wish to transfer to an alternative school they can accept their termination of employment as a result of redundancy and receive a compensatory redundancy payment.The Department has also agreed a reorganisation agreement which, subject to eligibility criteria detailed in DE Circular 2013/17, the salary of a teacher in a school closure is protected for four years if they are reengaged in an alternative grant aided school in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to not placing health boards or hospitals which do not meet diagnostic and cancer waiting times under recruitment freezes.
answer_text: Recruitment across HSC is the responsibility of individual HSC Trusts. The Department currently has no plans for recruitment freezes for any professions across the HSC.It is worth acknowledging the significant progress made in recent years to stabilise and grow the HSC workforce.  Since my Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy in 2018, there has been a 17.7% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC.  That amounts to 10,062 additional whole time equivalent staff across the HSC, including 1,019 (+24.3%) more medical and dental staff, 2,938 (+19.4%) more registered nurses and midwives and 2,026 (+24.9%) more professional and technical staff.These increases in staffing numbers have been supported by the strategic approach to workforce development outlined in the Workforce Strategy including strategic workforce planning, increased commissioning of pre- and post-registration training, better attraction and recruitment of staff, reducing agency dependency and developing initiatives to improve staff retention.  While we have made important strides in stabilising and growing our workforce, the positive impact of additional staffing is not felt uniformly across the HSC.  Key workforce challenges remain in particular services and locations.  My Department is committed to addressing these challenges through continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy and will shortly publish the Strategy’s third action plan which will outline priorities identified for progression over the next three years.  Continued implementation of the Workforce Strategy will require sustained, increased funding to ensure we provide the investment we know is required to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the 27 per cent increase in radiology outsourcing expenditure between the financial years 2022-23 and 2023-24; and (ii) what plans he has to address this increase.
answer_text: The increased cost of radiology outsourcing expenditure has been driven primarily by growing demand across radiology services. Ongoing difficulties in recruiting radiologists has impacted reporting capacity while CT Scanning in particular has seen demand increase by 10% annually, adding further pressures to in-house capacity. Outsourcing enables services to continue to provide opportunities for patients to receive scans.  However, reliance on costly outsourcing of radiology reporting will only be reduced by further increasing in-house capacity and maximising the benefits of recent investment to modernise the IT infrastructure underpinning imaging services.As the local radiology training scheme is at capacity, my Department plans to establish a multi-professional NI Imaging Academy to provide a regional solution that will significantly increase capacity within the Health and Social Care system for training greater numbers of radiographers and radiologists each year. The Academy will provide a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing. Progressing this initiative remains subject to business case approval and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to ensure that the current shortfall of radiologists does not increase to 41 per cent, or 122 consultants, by 2029, as forecast by the Royal College of Radiologists.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the current radiology workforce deficit and is working to address this.  Significant investment in growing the future imaging workforce in Northern Ireland has been made over the last ten years with commissioned radiology medical specialty training places being expanded from 37 (2015/16) to 55 (2024/25).  Despite the current pressures on my Department’s budget, I have been able to increase that training programme by another place for 2025/26 (i.e. to 56 places).Given that the local radiology training scheme is now at maximum capacity, my Department is currently developing the business case for a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy with the aim of significantly increasing further the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners into HSC funded imaging services.  Progress is subject to business case approval and the availability of funding.My Department has no plans to increase further the local clinical oncology medical specialty training programme.

Despite the current pressures on my Department’s budget, I have been able to increase that training programme by another place for 2025/26 (i.e. to 56 places).Given that the local radiology training scheme is now at maximum capacity, my Department is currently developing the business case for a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy with the aim of significantly increasing further the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners into HSC funded imaging services.  Progress is subject to business case approval and the availability of funding.My Department has no plans to increase further the local clinical oncology medical specialty training programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an update on Small Pockets of Deprivation funding after 30th of June 2025.
answer_text: My officials are preparing a Memorandum of Understanding for the period 01 April 2025 to 31 March 2026 for issue to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who administer the Small Pockets of Deprivation funding on behalf of my Department. The allocation for the programme of £507,305.32 reflects a 9% increase on the 2024/25 award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline where lithium batteries, as used in mobility aids, can be safely disposed of in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: It may be possible to dispose of lithium batteries at local council Household Recycling Centres (HRCs) operating waste battery disposal schemes. The local council should be contacted directly for guidance.If any further information is required on battery disposal, please contact NIEA’s Hazardous Waste Team 028 9056 9710 / hazwaste@daera-ni.gov.uk
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on replacing lanterns with LED lanterns and individual photocells on the Carrickmannon Road, Ballygowan, in order to conserve energy and save money, given that these lanterns are currently switched on 24 hours a day.
answer_text: My Department is aware of several streetlights on the Carrickmannon Road that are lit continuously as a result of Northern Ireland Electricity upgrade works in the area. Whilst my Department does not incur additional lighting charges at times when the lights would normally be off, from an environmental perspective, it is important that the issue is rectified as soon as possible.   Officials are working with their street lighting contractor to progress the installation of new LED lanterns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recruitment of Disability Social Workers for the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have benefitted from the regional social work recruitment process which takes place twice a year, the graduate recruitment process which takes place annually and have also held bespoke recruitment events to recruit to areas of specific need. Currently there are no vacancies in adult physical disability services. Adult learning disability services have reduced vacancies from 9.48wte to 4.8wte. There are three teams within Children’s Disability Services, one based in each sector, Lisburn, Newtownards and Down. There are currently no vacancies in the Down sector, the Lisburn sector has one social work vacancy and one senior social work practitioner vacancy and Newtownards have one social work vacancy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the plans to progress the School Enhancement Programme for Killyleagh Integrated Primary School, which was due to commence in 2018.
answer_text: Killyleagh Integrated Primary School was announced in May 2018 to advance in design under the Second Call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2).  The business case for the project was approved in April 2024 by my department.The Education Authority (EA) is currently reviewing the proposed design to ensure that the project can deliver a cost-effective solution that meets the needs of the school.Funding is available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional funding for education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale why mobility aid equipment given to patients following injury or surgery cannot be returned to his Department or hospital when no longer necessary for cleaning and reuse in order to reduce the waste of resources.
answer_text: Mobility aid equipment given to patients labelled as “single use” cannot be re-used as this would breach the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA).  The cost of decontamination and cleaning in line with infection control guidelines is not cost effective.In hospital/outpatient settings equipment can be re-used after short-term use.  For more expensive pieces of equipment, e.g. wheeled walkers, these can be repurposed as they are not significantly challenged supporting excessive weights limits and are not at risk from structural damage.A number of HSC trusts have equipment recycling programmes in place where equipment can be returned, recycled and reused when possible.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the projected 2025-26 budget to be allocated to local councils for dedicated animal welfare activity.
answer_text: There is no budget allocated within the 2025-26 budget for local councils in relation to animal welfare. However, I have asked that my officials provide limited funding for training of more dog wardens, in local councils, on handling of dangerous dogs.
