question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of the National Joint Council national pay award for Housing Executive employees.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Following receipt of approval to implement the NJC pay award, staff pay was uplifted to the revised spinal points in February and backpay is included in March pay.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to introduce a centralised, publicly-accessible list of all registered and licensed breeders and sellers of dogs and puppies.
answer_text: It is my intention to consult on proposals for a version of Lucys Law in Northern Ireland. This would seek to not only ban the third-party sale of pups and kittens but also introduce requirements which would improve traceability and transparency, enabling the public to more easily identify the source and origin of their puppy or kitten. This is one of my priority animal welfare reforms within my forthcoming Animal Welfare Pathway. Work is ongoing on the consultation, and it would be my intention that this launches in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of funding allocated to animal welfare departments in local councils in 2024-25.
answer_text: No funding was allocated by my Department to local councils during the 2024-25 financial year for the purposes of animal welfare.  My Department has, however, allocated £90k to local councils in 24/25 towards the costs of enforcing the new legal requirements in relation to XL Bully type dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to the financial benefits that can be unlocked by investing in Auditory Verbal therapy and expanding access to the support for deaf children and their families.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is actively engaged in addressing the needs of the speech and language therapy workforce, including considerations around Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT). The Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 has evaluated the workforce required to deliver services across Northern Ireland, acknowledging increased demands on these services since the review.In response to these growing needs, the Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions was reconvened in March 2024. The workforce subcommittee of this Advisory Committee has been tasked with considering all workforce issues for Allied Health Professions. This includes a critical review of the recommendations from the Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 to inform future workforce requirements.Department officials are currently considering the demand/capacity for audiology services in NI. There is a regional Allied Health Professional (AHP) Education Commissioning Group (ECG), led by the Department, which identifies and prioritises the allocation of funding for education and training programmes for AHPs across health and social care, and this includes speech and language therapy. The ECG has representation from senior AHPs across all HSC Trusts and regional HSC organisations who have extensive knowledge of service-user needs, trends and service demands. It ensures that decisions made on the training priorities across the AHP group results in the commissioning of programmes that reflect service need, are value for money, achieve return on investment and deliver enhanced patient outcomes. It would therefore be appropriate that any future consideration of funding for Auditory Verbal training is considered through this regular ECG process, as with any other requests, to ensure consistency in approach. As with all service-user clinical presentations that require speech and language therapy, there are a range of different options and treatment modalities required to meet the needs of deaf children and their families. The regional ECG forum takes due cognisance of this and subsequently prioritises training programmes that meets these needs. This ensures therapists have the necessary tools, approaches, and strategies to meet the varying needs of the service-user and their family. My Department has reached out to clinical colleagues for some initial input on Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT) for non-hearing children and while they have suggested that there is a lack of well-controlled studies addressing the use of AVT, they have also suggested that this would not necessarily suggest AVT may be an ineffective intervention in the habilitation of children with cochlear implants.  Therefore, more information is required but this will be reviewed/agreed by clinicians in due course. AVUK therapy is not a currently commissioned service within Northern Ireland and to date there has been no consideration given to the financial benefits that can be unlocked by investing in Audito

My Department has reached out to clinical colleagues for some initial input on Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT) for non-hearing children and while they have suggested that there is a lack of well-controlled studies addressing the use of AVT, they have also suggested that this would not necessarily suggest AVT may be an ineffective intervention in the habilitation of children with cochlear implants.  Therefore, more information is required but this will be reviewed/agreed by clinicians in due course. AVUK therapy is not a currently commissioned service within Northern Ireland and to date there has been no consideration given to the financial benefits that can be unlocked by investing in Audito

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking, alongside clinicians, to increase access to Auditory Verbal therapy for deaf children and their families.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is actively engaged in addressing the needs of the speech and language therapy workforce, including considerations around Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT). The Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 has evaluated the workforce required to deliver services across Northern Ireland, acknowledging increased demands on these services since the review.In response to these growing needs, the Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions was reconvened in March 2024. The workforce subcommittee of this Advisory Committee has been tasked with considering all workforce issues for Allied Health Professions. This includes a critical review of the recommendations from the Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 to inform future workforce requirements.Department officials are currently considering the demand/capacity for audiology services in NI. There is a regional Allied Health Professional (AHP) Education Commissioning Group (ECG), led by the Department, which identifies and prioritises the allocation of funding for education and training programmes for AHPs across health and social care, and this includes speech and language therapy. The ECG has representation from senior AHPs across all HSC Trusts and regional HSC organisations who have extensive knowledge of service-user needs, trends and service demands. It ensures that decisions made on the training priorities across the AHP group results in the commissioning of programmes that reflect service need, are value for money, achieve return on investment and deliver enhanced patient outcomes. It would therefore be appropriate that any future consideration of funding for Auditory Verbal training is considered through this regular ECG process, as with any other requests, to ensure consistency in approach. As with all service-user clinical presentations that require speech and language therapy, there are a range of different options and treatment modalities required to meet the needs of deaf children and their families. The regional ECG forum takes due cognisance of this and subsequently prioritises training programmes that meets these needs. This ensures therapists have the necessary tools, approaches, and strategies to meet the varying needs of the service-user and their family. My Department has reached out to clinical colleagues for some initial input on Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT) for non-hearing children and while they have suggested that there is a lack of well-controlled studies addressing the use of AVT, they have also suggested that this would not necessarily suggest AVT may be an ineffective intervention in the habilitation of children with cochlear implants.  Therefore, more information is required but this will be reviewed/agreed by clinicians in due course. AVUK therapy is not a currently commissioned service within Northern Ireland and to date there has been no consideration given to the financial benefits that can be unlocked by investing in Audito

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department's policy on road markings at junctions; and (ii) what is acceptable in order to ensure safety to road users.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department and we are fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted.Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is ineffective or worn away. Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits. Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings, such as STOP and NO ENTRY markings, and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will carry out repairs to the road markings on the Comber Road, Saintfield, where it meets the Belfast Road.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department and we are fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted.Maintenance of traffic signs and road markings are generally concentrated on regulatory signs and markings that are necessary for continued road safety. Additional road markings are considered for renewal subject to the availability of funding.                                                                                       My officials will assess the road markings on the Comber Road, Saintfield at this location in line with current policy and, if they meet the current intervention level, will add them to a future programme of work subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on actions aimed at addressing waiting times for elective surgery for inflammatory bowel disease-related illnesses.
answer_text: The Review of General Surgery (2022) and the Elective Care Framework (2024) provides the strategic context for the delivery of equitable and timely surgery for all general surgical conditions including IBD.IBD conditions are managed in secondary care by both Gastroenterologists and General (Colorectal) Surgeons. Close collaboration between Gastroenterology and Colorectal teams, multidisciplinary team working and maximising opportunities for non-surgical management have been fundamental in improving outcomes for patients with bowel disease-related illnesses.Regrettably the draft budget provides zero additional funding for waiting list reduction initiatives. A series of bids made in 2024/25 were also all unfunded. Reducing waiting times for elective surgery is a priority for my Department, the Health and Social Care Sector and the Northern Ireland Executive however, reducing our elective waiting lists requires levels of investment which the current draft budget does not contain.  I am determined to do whatever I can, within the budget I have available to me, to improve the waiting list position across all specialities, including IBD surgery.  This includes maximising theatre capacity, driving efficiency, cross Trust working and reforming service delivery via our elective care centres.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they intend to take forward the findings of the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report in conjunction with other relevant departments.
answer_text: The scope of the findings of High Street Task Force and its recommendations lie within the responsibilities of a number of departments.On 10th October 2024 we therefore wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.Responses were received from all four departments, outlining how they would take forward recommendations that fell within their responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether greater collaboration can be achieved with local government on relevant priorities within the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's policy on the maintenance and repair of community defibrillators.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of accessing community-based defibrillators, without delay, in saving lives of people experiencing cardiac arrest in the community.  All Public Access Defibrillators (PAD’s) should be registered on The Circuit, which is the National Defibrillator Network. Part of this process involves assigning a guardian, who could be a member of the public, a representative of a business, a club or school etc. The guardian is responsible for maintenance, upkeep and checks of these devices to ensure that they are regularly inspected and kept ‘emergency-ready’. The guardian is supported by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and The Circuit.Regular, automated emails are sent from The Circuit as a reminder to guardians to perform necessary checks on the defibrillators and associated equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made on regional inequalities in diabetes care across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health launched the Diabetes Strategic Framework, in November 2016. The Northern Ireland Diabetes Network was established to delivery against the framework.   In partnership with stakeholders, including people living with diabetes, the Network has been working to coproduce solutions and regional pathways.  Over the past three years, the Northern Ireland Diabetes Network has undertaken both qualitative and quantitative research in the development of a Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes.  Working with all key stakeholders, the Northern Ireland Diabetes Network will continue to work to support people living with diabetes and improving outcomes in diabetes treatment and care.One example of this is the Regional Insulin Pump service, as a commissioned clinical model.  By improving equity and reduced variability, patients will receive better clinical care, improved quality of care and outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made on the importance of providing diabetes patients with greater choice of treatment, particularly through the development of advanced continuous glucose monitoring technologies.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's plans to implement the 5.5 per cent pay rise that has been recommended by the Pay Review Body for health workers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Ahead of the NI Executive monitoring round, my Department was faced with a budget deficit in terms of available funding to meet the cost of maintaining core health and social care services at current levels.Despite an unprecedented level of savings of £200m implemented across HSC, the allocation of £350m in October monitoring, while welcome, has still left a funding gap of £100m to meet service pressures and pay uplifts.Last week I met with Trade Unions representing Agenda for Change health staff and firm proposals on a pay deal have been put to them, which they will now consider. It is important to recognise that further work still needs to be undertaken in order to reach a resolution to the issue of health service pay. It is also important to recognise that the challenges facing public sector pay in general will only be resolved through an all-executive approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what steps his Department can take to encourage greater engagement with the Digital Schoolhouse programme among controlled schools.
answer_text: The integration of digital skills into all areas of the Northern Ireland Curriculum is important both to individual learners and to wider society.  Equipping young people with digital proficiency is imperative for their future success.  My Department has prioritised digital skills as an area of focus within the curriculum and is working with colleagues in the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to develop actions which aim to enhance and integrate digital skills across all areas, at all key stages.  This will include highlighting examples of best practice to schools.CCEA is responsible for producing resources to support schools and it has previously signposted teachers to the Digital Schoolhouse website, as a potential source of information.Schools here have the autonomy to decide which resources or programmes they use to support delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans his Department has to mark the 250th anniversary of America's Declaration of Independence, in 2026.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her discussions with HM Treasury on the review of the Northern Ireland funding formula.
answer_text: In total, the application of the 124% needs-based factor from the Interim Fiscal Framework has resulted in an additional £431.4 million of additional funding for the Executive.  This demonstrates that the Interim Fiscal Framework, which I agreed on behalf of the Executive in May, has and continues to deliver for people by providing hundreds of millions of pounds in additional funding for public services hereI am in regular contact with my Ministerial counterparts in the Treasury, pressing on the need for a fair funding settlement for the North, including agreeing the mechanics of the application of the needs-based factor going forward, the calculation of our relative funding per head, and the approach to reviewing our level of need.I met with both the Chief Secretary to Treasury and the Chancellor in mid-July. Then, along with the First and Deputy First Ministers, I met with the Chancellor on 12 September. I also met the Chief Secretary at the Interministerial Finance Committee on 3 October alongside counterparts from Scotland and Wales, where I had a direct bilateral meeting with him. In all of these engagements, and in separate correspondence, I continue to push for the full implementation of the commitments within the Interim Fiscal Framework.We will continue to move at pace to deliver our commitments as set out in the Interim Fiscal Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans in place to retain (i) emergency department staff; and (ii) ambulance staff.
answer_text: My Department published the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People in May 2018.  This outlines a programme of work aimed at making the HSC a fulfilling and rewarding place to work and train where our people feel valued and supported.  A summary report on progress towards implementation of the Strategy’s actions (to the end of March 2024) is published on the Department’s website: HSC Workforce Strategy 2026 Summary Report on Progress to 31 March 2024.pdf (health-ni.gov.uk)HSC Trusts, including the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, provide a range of initiatives that support retention of staff in emergency care settings.  These include: Opportunities for professional development, learning and career progression, including active teams of clinical educators and the development of Advanced nurse and Allied Health Professional practitioner roles in emergency care settingsSupport for newly qualified staff, emergency care focussed induction programmes and dedicated peer support networks for emergency care staffProvision of staff health and wellbeing programmes including psychological support services with staff actively encouraged to avail of health and wellbeing supportCelebrating and recognising publicly the work undertaken within emergency service settings and the challenges these staff face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of frontline emergency staff positions that are currently vacant.
answer_text: Information on HSC staff in post and vacancies actively being recruited by staff group and organisation is published by my Department on a quarterly basis at the link below. The latest information available refers to the position on 30 June 2024.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/dhssps-statistics-and-research/workforce-statisticsThere is no definition of frontline staff, however at 30 June 2024 there were 61 vacancies actively being recruited in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), 31 more than 30 June 2023, and 55 less than 30 June 2019. These vacancies range from administrative and support staff, to control workers, to paramedics and emergency medical technicians (EMTs).At 30 June 2024, there were 1,434 whole-time equivalent (WTE) staff employed in NIAS.  This is a decrease of 58 WTE compared to 30 June 2023, but an increase of 170 WTE compared to 30 June 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to improve safety on the Portaferry Road, Newtownards, following a number of accidents over the last year.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to increase spend across General Practice from 5.4 per cent in the 2022-23 financial year.
answer_text: The NIAO report on access to General Practice, published in March 2024, included the NIAO’s calculation that only 5.4% of HSC spend in 2022/23 was directed towards General Practice.  The figure of £375m quoted in the NIAO report for investment in primary care was taken from the Department’s Report “Investment in General Practice’ published in October 2023. This figure is not fully comprehensive as it focuses on the elements of funding that are allocated directly to GP practices and GP Federations. It does not include elements of spend which are allocated to other HSC bodies, including Trusts, but which are investment in primary care. This would include a significant portion of spend in the Primary Care MDT Programme, as well as the investment in the Primary Care Elective Programme and work on No More Silos. The total value of this additional investment in Primary Care which is not captured in the £375m figure is £19.5m. Investment in the General Practice Pharmacy scheme is captured in the £375m figure quoted.	I recognise that General Practice plays a pivotal role within our health and social care system and my Department is continuing to take measures to secure improved sustainability of General Practice provision.  The issues that are impacting General Practice are complex and will require a sustained long-term approach to address.As I have made clear, I would like to rebalance how HSC services are delivered, to facilitate a greater proportion of care to be provided in primary and community care, closer to people’s homes and with less reliance on secondary care. My recently announced “Live Better” pilot scheme is an important part of that agenda, seeking to take services right into the heart of the community making a positive difference to people’s lives. I also want to be able to further invest in the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams, so more people can benefit from prompt access to a range of support across primary care. Accordingly, a bid for transformation funding to support the further rollout of the MDT Programme across the five-year span of the transformation fund has been submitted to the Department of Finance.  As you know the 2024-25 Budget outcome was extremely disappointing for Health and the additional funding provided was significantly less than what we required and has resulted in 2.3% less funding in 2024-25 than we spent in 2023-24. This has left health with some extremely difficult decisions manage within the limited budget allocated and opportunities for increased investment in General Practice must be considered in this context.Despite the continuing challenging budgetary position, I remain committed to supporting General Practice services so that they can continue to provide vital services to our communities both now, and in the future.In 2023/24, in response to the recommendation of the report of the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration (DDRB) published in July 2023, an uplift of 6% (net of expenses) was

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to create a workforce plan to support General Practice provision.
answer_text: Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department has continued its focus on building the GP workforce.  This includes funding to support the recruitment and retention of GPs through a number of initiatives comprising the “Attract, Recruit, Retain, Scheme” the GP Retainer Scheme, the GP Mentor Scheme, the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme and the GP Fellowship Scheme.  My Department has taken action to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland and we are currently updating the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher schemes to align with the new General Medical Council (GMC) pathways for the recruitment of GPs from other countries joining the GMC GP Register.  My Department has also introduced a Streamlined Returner process with the aim of removing unnecessary barriers to those wishing to return to the GP workforce.   Over recent years, my Department has prioritised investment in the training of future GPs.  The annual number of GP training places has been increased from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  In addition, my Department is working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) on innovations to the local GP training programme with the objective of enhancing the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.The Ulster University’s School of Medicine at Magee Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on primary care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west of Northern Ireland.  It is hoped that this will help ensure a supply of local students who wish to pursue a career in General Practice in these areas.My Department has also been taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists.  It is clear that GPs benefit from the MDT model in helping their ability to meet ever growing demands and pressures.  While I want to be able to invest more in primary care Multi-Disciplinary Teams to facilitate further roll out, current budgetary constraints make this very difficult.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how schools are assessed for eligibility for refurbishment projects.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to provide support and information to people affected by the supply of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapies, such as people with cystic fibrosis.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of supply chains to the UK, and works closely with the other devolved administrations, including my Department, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and other operators within the supply chain. Through this engagement and UK-wide medicines shortages working groups, my Department is aware of ongoing UK-wide supply disruptions affecting pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) treatments. There are national and locally tried and tested mitigations that can be used during periods of supply disruption. Several of these steps have been taken in response to supply disruptions to PERT products.A National Patient Safety Alert was issued by my Department on 24 May 2024, providing healthcare professionals with advice and actions to take to ensure patients can continue to access appropriate treatments. This alert has superseded previous guidance issued to healthcare professionals through Medicines Supply Notifications. Healthcare professionals can also register with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) which provides up-to-date information on medicine shortages and their anticipated resupply, and SPS has a dedicated webpage providing advice on the prescribing and ordering of available PERT products.Additionally, PERT products were added to the UK restricted export and hoarding list on 22 May 2024, and Serious Shortage Protocols (SSPs) have been issued to community pharmacists for Creon® 10,000 and 25,000 capsules. My Department will continue to work closely with DHSC and the other devolved administrations on these UK-wide supply disruptions to help ensure patients and/ or their representatives can continue to have access to appropriate treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Cystic Fibrosis Trust report that 74 per cent of people with the condition in Northern Ireland had worked despite being too unwell, to detail the steps being taken to provide additional (i) financial; and (ii) practical support to these people.
answer_text: Depending on individual circumstances, a person with cystic fibrosis may have access to certain disability and/or income-related benefits in the social security system.  To ensure a person is in receipt of all the Financial support they are entitled to, they should contact the Department for Communities’ Make the Call Service via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.The adult cystic fibrosis service is a regional service that works with people from all over Northern Ireland.  The service also works closely with the paediatric cystic fibrosis team and includes: specialists’ consultants and doctors, dieticians, nurses, social workers etc.  The social work team develops person-centred, biopsychosocial working relationships with cystic fibrosis (CF) patients and their families.  They:empower patients and promote their right to self-determine all aspects of their life.tailor an individual social assessment to the patient’s needs.consider all the patient’s care and support needs, and any risks that may be present in their life, including those related to child protection and adult safeguarding.Social workers also seek to support carers.  They offer carer assessments and make referrals for various therapeutic supports and carer grants.In addition, the social work team helps families and patients deal with education and employment opportunities. They offer support and advice on securing state benefits, which gives patients greater financial security and improves their quality of life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the sectors where there is a skills shortage within the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: Question 11 – AQO 939/22-27The latest economic evidence shows that the local labour market continues to remain very challenging with an annual undersupply of 4,300 workers, unemployment remaining historically low at 2.0%, economic inactivity remaining stubbornly high at 27.5%, and many sectors reporting recruitment challenges.In terms of the various sectors in our economy, the 2022 Employer Skills Survey reported 13,700 skills shortages which was double the amount recorded in 2019. The report also highlighted that the industries with the highest level of skill-shortage vacancies included Transport and Storage, Construction, Primary Sector Utilities and Manufacturing.This is a key challenge that I am fully committed to addressing as set out in my Skills statement at the beginning of July.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures his Department has taken to combat flooding, such as that seen in August 2023 in Dromara, Co Down.
answer_text: To alleviate the impacts of flooding from rivers, my Department’s Rivers Directorate plans to continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourse and their associated infrastructure within the Dromara area. My officials, along with Local Government, will continue to work closely with the established community resilience group in Dromara to help them prepare for, and respond to, severe weather events including flooding. Additional resources have been provided in the area which can be accessed by the local resilience group members.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement his Department has had with NI Water on a co-ordinated response to flooding in Dromara, Co Down.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from NI Water, and I have been advised that staff from DfI Roads, DfI Rivers and NI Water attended a meeting on 26 February 2024, at Dromara Community Centre. This meeting was organised by Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council and included local community representatives to discuss the flooding issues the village had experienced.I am aware that NI Water informed the meeting attendees that NI Water had conducted investigations and a CCTV survey within its infrastructure and found no faults.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the action he is taking to address the issue of 16 year-olds to 24 year-olds not in education, employment or training.
answer_text: Since 2013, the rate of young people aged 16 to 24 in the North who are not in education, employment or training (NEET) has halved, and now sits at 7%. 	A targeted approach is now necessary for the persistent 7-8%, who are likely to be experiencing additional challenges, such as having Special Educational Needs (SEN) and / or disabilities, be care experienced, have addiction / mental health issues, live in areas of deprivation, or be newcomer families	My department is delivering a range of relevant interventions, including the establishment of a new SEN project team, and a number of skills programmes, including some which have elements specifically targeting those who are NEET, for example, Multiply, Skill Up, and Step Up, which support individuals in accessing training suited to their own circumstance.	Our Further Education (FE) colleges provide courses across a range of levels to support all young people wishing to enter education, training or employment. 	Furthermore, the Department’s student support package provides financial support to eligible students undertaking further education courses. Additional, targeted support is available for students who may require extra support during the course of their studies.

answer_text: The information you have requested is not collected or held by my Department.  GP Partners in a General Medical Services Practice are Independent Contractors rather than employees within the Health and Social Care System. GMS Contractors may employ salaried GPs according to the needs of their practice.However, all doctors who wish to work as a GP in Northern Ireland are required to be included in the ‘Northern Ireland Primary Medical Performers List (PMPL). question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the normal contractual hours of a GP regarded as working a full week in the health service.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not collected or held by my Department.  GP Partners are contractors rather than employees of the Health and Social Care system.  A GP practice holding a contract to provide General Medical Services  (GMS) would be obliged to provide GMS services during core hours – the hours being worked by individual GPs would be an internal matter for the practice. The Statement of Financial Entitlement (element of the GMS contract) defines “full-time” in relation to a salaried GP within a practice as a contractual obligation to work for at least 37½ hours per normal working week. The total hours may be made up of surgeries, clinics, administrative work in connection with the performance of primary medical services, or management activities and other similar duties which enhance the performance of the contractor as a provider of primary medical services but do not directly relate to the performance of primary medical services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GPs contracted to work less than a full working week in the health service in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not collected or held by my Department.  GP Partners are Independent Contractors rather than employees within the Health and Social Care System.A  General Medical Service (GMS) contractor would be obliged to provide GMS services during core hours - defined within the GMS Contract as the period beginning at 8am and ending at 6.30pm on any day from Monday to Friday except bank holidays and other public or local holidays agreed with my Department .A GP practice may employ a salaried GP – in such cases the working hours are a matter for the employer.  All doctors who wish to work as GP in Northern Ireland are required to be included in the ‘Northern Ireland Primary Medical Performers List (PMPL).  For the 2024/25 financial year there are 1985 (or 2310 including trainees) registered GPs in NI.I am committed to building the GP workforce across Northern Ireland.  Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the workforce.  This includes funding the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme, the GP Mentor scheme, the GP Fellowship scheme, and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme.  The annual number of GP training places has been increased from 65 in 2015/16 to the current level of 121 (an increase of 86%) and steps have also been taken to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the ROI, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions he has had with the Department of Finance to ensure the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme can be funded beyond March 2025.
answer_text: I made a Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 setting out a £25m package of measures for early learning and childcare in 2024-25.  In that statement and the Executive Paper which underpinned it, I made it clear that this investment should not be seen as a single year commitment.  It is critical that the Executive takes forward the measures introduced in 2024-25 and expands upon them in future years.  Clearly, this will require increased funding in the next year and subsequent years, a point which I have repeatedly made to Executive colleagues, including the Minister for Finance.One of the measures I want to continue is the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, which seeks to ease the financial pressures on households and enable more parents to remain in work. I am delighted to say that this Scheme has already begun to deliver for working parents, with the first payments made in September 2024.  Executive colleagues are aware that they will need to invest in it again next year if it is to remain in place beyond March 2025.  While no decisions have yet been taken in respect of the next financial year, my Department is engaged with the future years financial planning exercise currently under way.  In so doing, my officials and I will continue to constructively engage with the Department of Finance and the Executive with regard to the budget for 2025-26.I can assure you that I will be making a strong case for the funding to sustain and expand early learning and childcare provision across a range of areas.  I also anticipate that we as an Executive will give priority to this area within the draft Programme for Government by ensuring that future years’ budgets prioritise investment in the Early Years and Childcare Strategy, including the Executive’s Childcare Subsidy Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what plans are in place to address rising levels of unpaid rates.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has robust billing, collection and recovery processes in place to maximise rate collection and reduce rating debt. This includes issuing annual rate bills and final demands for payment. Where an account is not in payment, they initiate recovery procedures including formal written correspondence, e-mails and direct telephone contact with ratepayers. Legal recovery actions are taken for persistent non-payment. This includes progression through the Magistrate Courts, the Enforcement of Judgement’s Office and bankruptcy proceedings. Ratepayers experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to contact LPS to discuss payment options which can include restructuring current payment plans or establishing longer term payment arrangements to enable payment of rate arrears

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps he will take to increase availability of renal psychology services for kidney patients; and (ii) how he plans to address recent reductions in the availability of this aspect of kidney care in (a) the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Specialist renal psychology services are commissioned for delivery in Belfast and Western Trust.  Referrals to the service are progressed in alignment with the outcome of the multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussions.  Developments beyond this specialist service will be informed by the 2021 -2031 Mental Health Strategy, which directs the service to provide whole person and whole system responses for patients with chronic conditions.  The suggested models to deliver this involve greater integration of psychology within MDTs and additional training for staff from other disciplines in the recognition and referral of patients.  SPPG will continue to work with Trust renal services to assess and map levels of psychological need to the range of available services including those provided closer to home through primary, community and voluntary sector provision.(ii)(a)	 Kidney Care UK provided non-recurrent funding for additional psychological support within the Western Trust which has now come to an end.  The Western Trust are seeking to develop a business case to expand the baseline commissioned service going forward.  SPPG will consider this option through the regional engagement arrangements with nephrology and transplantation stakeholders in the context of the Mental Health Strategy recommendations.(ii)(b) The Belfast Trust also had access to short term non-recurrent funding to support mental health care in addition to the commissioned baseline service.  This will also be considered through the regional engagement arrangements with nephrology and transplantation stakeholders in the context of the Mental Health Strategy recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to increase the availability of NHS dentists in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I acknowledge the challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it. For many months the Department has been trying to address these issues with increased funding.In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.The Department also announced in March a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Details on how to access the Dental Access Scheme can be found at https://online.hscni.net/our-work/dental-services/dental-access-scheme/.The Department will continue to consider ways to further support dental services in the coming months. However, the major obstacle to further investment is the budget, which you voted for.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress on the introduction of legislation concerning (i) the regulation and licensing of dog breeders; (ii) the regulation and licensing of animal welfare establishments, including rehoming organisations and sanctuaries; and (iii) boarding kennels.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward legislation on a range of important animal welfare issues, but I am also mindful of what can be delivered within the resources available to my Department and the time remaining in this mandate.Nevertheless, I will continue to engage with stakeholder organisations in the time ahead which will assist me in setting the key animal welfare priorities for the remainder of the mandate.  I am happy to explore the merits of introducing new licensing and regulatory requirements for those involved in dog breeding; the rescue and rehoming sector; and boarding kennels.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department intends to support allied health professionals to deliver new National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines for stroke rehabilitation, which now recommend at least three hours of therapy, five days a week for stroke survivors; and (ii) what consideration his Department will give to the use and roll-out of new technologies and innovations, such as virtual reality, to enhance intensity and frequency of stroke rehabilitation therapies.
answer_text: I recognise the important role of Allied Health Professionals in providing rehabilitation services for stroke patients. I understand that the recent changes in National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) will further impact on existing pressures within the stroke rehabilitation service. In the context of these pressures, my Department intends to take forward a Stroke Workforce Review later this year. The Review will identify the future workforce required to deliver a high-quality stroke service and will be informed by best practice guidance including the revised NICE guidelines on stroke rehabilitation.In the interim, my Department is supporting innovative ways of delivering stroke rehabilitation services, including a pilot of multidisciplinary team stroke rehabilitation groups in the South Eastern Trust. In addition, a pilot has also been introduced in the Belfast Trust to support the use of virtual reality in the delivery of Occupational Therapy. An app to support the delivery of Speech and Language Therapy is also currently in development. It is important to note that the introduction of such technology beyond the pilot phase is subject to additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Clinical Radiology and Clinical Oncology Workforce Census reports, for his assessment of the projected shortfalls of 44 per cent for clinical radiologists and 12 per cent for clinical oncologists, by 2028.
answer_text: I am aware of the workforce challenges that exist across health and social care.I welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ report. I have already agreed to meet representatives to discuss their findings in detail. The College is an important stakeholder in delivering effective cancer and imaging services for our population.  My Department is committed to the developing cancer and imaging services for the people of Northern Ireland.Following on from the Cancer Strategy, by this year we will have invested £10.88m to stabilise HSC Trusts’ oncology and haematology services.  This will increase the number of funded posts relating to oncology and haematology, including clinical oncologists.  Specific cancer pathways are currently being developed, which will inform future strategic workforce planning to address workforce requirements for clinical oncologists and other professions.  The active expansion of multi-disciplinary teams is a key part of our overarching programme to reform health and social care.With respect to Imaging Services, my Department and the HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase capacity within the health and social care system for training greater numbers of radiologists and radiographers each year, including interventional radiologist consultants.  It will provide a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.I remain profoundly dissatisfied with the level of funding being made available to my Department which has hampered and limited the Department’s ability to make further progress that can transform the delivery of health and social care in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on repair work to the roof of Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the Prisons 25by25 programme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s plans to complete the upgrade of the A7 Crossgar Road, Saintfield.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's assessment of the availability of specialist doctors and nurses for headaches in Northern Ireland, per capita, compared to other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I recognise that there are pressures including workforce challenges within Neurology, which is why my Department has been taking forward a Regional Review of Neurology Services. The Review is tasked with identifying the workforce and training needs required to underpin Neurology services going forward. The Review Team has undertaken an assessment of the baseline neurology workforce including the Specialist Consultant and Nursing workforces. This baseline has been benchmarked against recognised standards and guidelines including the Association of British Neurologists and The Royal College of Physicians guidelines. In the context of this assessment, there is a clear need to strengthen the overall workforce. In addition to an increase in numbers, the Review has also considered a range of measures to increase capacity. This includes the development of extended roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners and GPs with Enhanced Roles. This will help to ensure that people are seen in the right place at the right time and by the right person. It is expected that the implementation of the Review’s recommendations will drive improvements in neurology services which will benefit all neurology patients, including those with headache and migraine.Implementation of the recommendations of the Review will require significant additional funding over a multi-year period. This will be a considerable challenge in the context of pressures on my Department’s budget.I look forward to receiving the final report of the Review in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce a pathway for treatment for children suffering from the neuropsychiatric conditions PANS/PANDAS.
answer_text: I would first of all say that I recognise that this condition causes distress and concern for children and their families.  Although rare there has been increasing occurrences of presentations of Paediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated (PANDA) to paediatric services and Paediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS).Where a presentation occurs, the child will normally be seen at their local District General Hospital by a paediatrician who will assess the symptoms as there are usually a number of symptoms related to this.  The paediatrician will review each presentation and based on this may request additional support from the regional neurological service in BHSCT or from their local CAMHS service, amongst others in order to put in place a treatment plan.Treatment options will vary dependent on the clinical presentation and paediatricians would welcome more information to treat patients more effectively.  Plans are in place regionally to create a clinically led network that allows clinicians to share best practice and gain more understanding of new and emerging issues.  It is hoped that conditions such as PANS/PANDA will be included in this.  It must be noted, as there are also potentially neuropsychiatric implications with this disorder, there will be a need to engage with CAMHS colleagues in Trusts as they have a crucial role to play in care and treatment of children with this condition.We must recognise that there are significant demands on CAMHS services as well as other paediatric services across the HSC, which are impacting on increased waiting times and the SPPG are working with Trusts to identify improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the timeframe of the pilot scheme to film the delivery of judgments in the Court of Appeal.
answer_text: I welcome the pilot scheme in the Court of Appeal in Northern Ireland, initiated by the Lady Chief Justice, which concluded at the end of April 2024 having filmed the delivery of judgments in four sentencing appeals.The aim of the not-for broadcast pilot was to give broadcasters a better understanding of the technical challenges and feasibility of filming proceedings, and for the judiciary and court users to experience the impact of cameras in court.   The pilot has been evaluated by the Lady Chief Justice and I will be engaging with her to consider the results to help inform future legislative options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to facilitate additional clinics and consultations for patients currently on a hospital consultant waiting list.

To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to work with the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to alleviate waiting times for patient care in the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Following a review of Emergency Medicine in NI, my Department is taking forward a number of actions to improve pressures on the wider unscheduled care system to reduce overcrowding and delays in emergency departments.  This includes actions to reduce ambulance delays, improve patient flow through emergency departments and the wider hospital system, and to ensure timely discharge for patients who no longer require hospital care.  My Department recognises the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department. The South Eastern HSC Trust has been working with the Ambulance Service to put in place alternative pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to attending an emergency department, where this is appropriate.  A significant programme of work has been undertaken to provide alternative, appropriate services for people with Urgent Care Centres opened at Lagan Valley and the Downe Hospitals.  At the Ulster Hospital a Minor Injury Unit was opened in 2023 to provide urgent care to patients not requiring attendance at the emergency department.  This aims to ensure that emergency services are available for those who need them. Monthly performance meetings to review 4-hour metrics and the number of patients waiting more than 12 hours in the emergency department have been held with the South Eastern HSC Trust to monitor patient delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what discussions his Department has had with the Ministry of Defence to increase the number of contracts awarded to small to medium-sized enterprises in Northern Ireland.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the implementation of the recommendations in the High Street Task Force report on Delivering a 21st Century High Street.
answer_text: We recognise the important role High Streets play in our society in more ways than one. They drive the economy and also create shared spaces where society thrives.The Task Force Report was published in March 2022.  It is now up to individual departments how to take forward recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what action she will take to address payment delays to the legal profession.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to upgrade the Newtownards MOT Test Centre.
answer_text: The DVA has no immediate plans to upgrade the Newtownards test centre. HHHowever, in addition to the ongoing work at Hydebank and Mallusk, it is developing proposals for two further new test centres at Campsie and Enniskillen.Investment in these test centres and any other new or existing test centres will be subject to further consideration and funding availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his Department's plans to ensure pupils at Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Saintfield are provided with a school place after Primary Four.
answer_text: Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Belfast offers special education provision for children up to primary 4.   Oakwood’s new dual campus site at Saintfield opened this year and currently provides education for children in their pre-school and primary 1 years.  It is anticipated that Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Saintfield site will provide education for children across Early Years, Foundation Stage and Key Stage 1, the same as the Belfast site.  There are currently no plans to extend provision beyond primary 4 at this stage.  As with children on the Belfast site, following a child’s annual review at the agreed point of transition the Education Authority (EA) will work closely with parents to secure a new school placement to best meet their needs at that time.The EA is currently working with Oakwood School and Assessment Centre, Saintfield to progress agreed enhancement programmes of work for September 2024 and will continue to work with the school to ensure the site is maximised to its full capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what assessment has been made of the impact of the Back in Business rate support scheme.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of their Department to attract investment from new US businesses.

To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce a baby loss certificate for those who have experienced the loss of a baby prior to 24 weeks, in line with the Department of Health and Social Care in England.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the projected cost of implementing the Regional Dementia Care Pathway.
answer_text: The Regional Dementia Care Pathway published in 2018 offers a transformational service delivery model incorporating a primary care led approach working closely with secondary care dementia services to deliver assessment, diagnosis, treatment and management of dementia. The pathway represents the out-workings of a number of recommendations from the Regional Dementia Strategy “Improving Dementia Services in Northern Ireland” (2011) and is aimed at improving the services and support arrangements currently available for people living with dementia, their families and carers.The ambitious pathway will assist practitioners in the delivery of high quality, person-centred dementia care services from the point of initial engagement with service users through to the end of life stage of the dementia journey. The pathway acknowledges and includes provision for the needs of younger people (early onset dementia) and those with a learning disability who have dementia. The pathway presents a fully integrated approach with services being delivered by a range of professionals in a range of settings including hospital and community care settings. The pathway presents an exciting opportunity for the development of new roles within primary care and the need for a competent, highly skilled workforce will be pivotal to the successful implementation of the pathway.At the time of publication of the pathway in early 2018, a crude estimate of costs of implementation of the pathway was circa £12M. The projected current cost for implementation would be considerably in excess of this amount due to increased costs over the intervening six-year period including inflationary costs. The London School of Economics and Political Science in November 2019 published projected prevalence and associated costs of dementia care in the UK  2019- 2040. The report concluded that Northern Ireland would see the highest increase in the number of people with dementia with the numbers rising by 95% over the 21-year period.  The report concluded that the total costs of dementia care in Northern Ireland in 2019 amounted to £0.8bn  with social care accounting for 42% of the total costs and unpaid care accounting for 43% of total costs.It is recognised that the lack of consistent support means people living with dementia are at greater risk of crisis which has an adverse impact on them, their carers and NHS Services. Dementia can be complex and involve symptoms that need tailored support. This means care providers often charge a premium rate for dementia care with average additional costs of 15% more than standard social care but in some cases, care can be up to 40% more expensive. Such care costs have not been factored into the costs of the regional dementia care pathway and would be additional costs for the health and social care system. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what discussions she has had with the banking sector to promote banking hubs in towns impacted by bank closures.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the work that has been undertaken with regional colleges to develop and promote apprenticeship programmes.
answer_text: The further education colleges, alongside a network of private training organisations and local universities, are an important part of the delivery model for apprenticeships. 		The FE colleges offer that sub-regional link to the Department’s apprenticeship programmes, providing strong linkages with businesses which allows them to help meet skills needs at a local level.  These strong links are particularly helpful in supporting the Department’s wider development and promotion of apprenticeships, for example during the annual Apprenticeship Week.		The colleges also provide direct support to a network of employer-led Sectoral Partnerships which ensure that the content of Traineeships, Apprenticeships and Higher Level Apprenticeships meets the needs of industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the recent announcements to close the (i) Danske Bank, Saintfield; and (ii) Ulster Bank, Ballynahinch, what plans are in place to facilitate banking for this rural community.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about the impact of bank branch closures on local communities here, particularly in our rural towns and villages, and for those customers that rely more heavily on in-person banking. I have therefore written to senior management in each of the main local banks operating here to call for an urgent roundtable meeting with them to discuss this matter. While the regulation of financial services rests with the British Treasury and the Financial Conduct Authority, I will be pressing the banks for access to vital financial services to be maintained right across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to (i) reintroduce the Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service Plus; and (ii) permanently fund it.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service Plus (NI PEARS Plus) pilot commenced in 2023/24 and ran for thirty-three weeks, with 834 patients accessing the community optometry-based service with acute eye conditions which would otherwise have presented at Belfast HSC Trust Eye Casualty. NI PEARS Plus seeks to build on the successful NI PEARS scheme which currently manages ca. 4,000 patients in community optometry settings, closer to home.As per commissioning specification, the pilot is currently being evaluated for impact, assessing metrics around activity, performance, clinical safety, patient experience and cost-effectiveness.Following evaluation, and in consultation with stakeholders, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) will make a commissioning decision on whether the service should be mainstreamed and introduced regionally, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what financial package will be provided to support farmers whose crops were damaged by flooding in November 2023.
answer_text: From an agriculture perspective, the impact of the flooding in early November 2023 was most acutely experienced by potato growers in counties Armagh and Down. At the time of the flooding, approximately 50% of the potato crop in the region remained unharvested and was at risk of loss due to water damage.Officials completed a business case to examine the need and value for money of a financial support scheme for potato growers. This concluded that the economic case for government intervention was not strong and unlikely to deliver a different or better outcome for the economy to that which would accrue in the absence of a support scheme. For this reason, a financial package has not been implemented. I acknowledge that the financial impact of this weather event on some farm businesses could be significant and I intend to review the impact of this flooding event. I look forward to engaging with key stakeholders to explore the case for financial support to affected farm businesses and whether there are options within the rules governing the use of public money that can be advanced.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans she has to increase the provision of school places in Comber, given the expansion of the village.
answer_text: Operational responsibility for the planning of education provision lies with the two statutory planning authorities, the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), who conduct area planning activity in consultation with a number of sectoral support bodies.In the development and delivery of its Strategic Area Plan (SAP), the EA monitors growth across all areas in Northern Ireland on an annual basis.  It assesses provision to identify areas of growth and, conversely, areas of decrease and to scope actions for progression.  As part of that process it assesses Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) live birth statistics as well as current and projected school age population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to bolster the provision of neo-natal bereavement care in Northern Ireland with additional resource allocation.
answer_text: All healthcare staff working in maternity and neonatal services in Northern Ireland follow the Department of Health’s Regional Bereavement Guidance on evidence-based, holistic care of parents and their families after the experience of miscarriage, stillbirth or neonatal death. This guidance establishes a regional standard of care throughout Northern Ireland. Pathways are in place for the emotional and practical support of parents who experience a neonatal death.  The team involved in providing care to the bereaved woman will work with her and the family to navigate the legal, procedural and emotional challenges following the death of a baby.  In addition, each Trust has a bereavement midwife resource to provide additional support to parents and families who have lost a baby through miscarriage, stillbirth or where their baby has died shortly after birth. Bereavement midwives also offer support and training to other members of staff to ensure that they can provide support to parents and families in these challenging circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to produce a workforce plan to ensure safe and sustainable staffing across maternity services.
answer_text: Effective workforce planning is a key theme in the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People (May 2018) and its Second Action Plan (2022-23 to 2024-25) (June 2022).The Second Action Plan contains a range of actions that address attraction, recruitment and training of staff, as well as the identification of our required workforce skills and supply over a five to ten year horizon through a rolling programme of strategic workforce reviews.  These reviews, which are led by my Department in partnership with stakeholders, will increasingly focus on planning by programme of care.  Aligned to the health and social care Transformation Programme, they will aim to address the multidisciplinary and interprofessional aspects of service delivery and training.The outputs of these service level workforce reviews provide a regional view of future workforce need and skills mix within a health and social care wide optimum workforce model – our strategic workforce plan.Regarding maternity services, my Department is planning their transformation to a Continuity of Midwifery Carer (CoMC) model.  Implementing this model in our maternity services will be crucial to ensuring that women, babies and families have the best and safest midwifery care possible.  Following implementation, we intend that continuity of midwifery carer will be available for all women, including those women who are pregnant following a previous pregnancy loss or the death of a baby.  Significant investment in staff and resources will be required, including the appointment of a Regional CoMC Midwife Consultant and a CoMC Implementation Lead Midwife in each Health and Social Care Trust to allow for coordination.My Department’s Chief Nursing Officer has also commissioned Phase 11 of the Delivering Care Policy Framework, to determine a safe and effective staffing model for the midwifery workforce in Northern Ireland.  This work is progressing within the Public Health Agency, and we are endeavouring to propose a workforce model and requirements for midwifery by the end of 2022. The Executive agreed on 14 January 2020 to provide additional funding of £5-10m in 2020/21, increasing thereafter to £60m over 5 years, to deal with safe staffing concerns, including the full implementation of Delivering Care; although the Department recognises that there may be a need for additional funding within that timeframe.This work complements my undertaking in the 2020 Framework Agreement to develop and introduce robust Safe Staffing legislation with the agreement of Trade Union Side, HSC Employers and our Chief Professional Officers.  My Department’s commitment to introducing a Bill early in the new Assembly mandate remains, despite current uncertainties.Growing the local supply of healthcare workers, such as midwives, depends upon the availability of significant, sustained, recurrent investment in pre- and post-registration education and training.
